content:
8 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

It’s not the prompt engineers. It’s not the AI influencers. It’s not even the developers. The people quietly cleaning up from AI right now share one trait almost nobody is talking about.
A wedding photographer in Austin told me she doubled her income last year. Not from shooting more weddings. She shot fewer. She raised her prices by 40%, started delivering galleries in 72 hours instead of three weeks, and began offering AI-edited preview albums within 24 hours of the event. Her editing time dropped from 30 hours per wedding to 6. She kept all the margin. She told me she doesn’t consider herself an “AI person.” She just added one tool to a skill she already had and let the math do the rest.
Nobody wrote an article about her. Nobody put her on a podcast about the future of AI. She doesn’t have a course on how she did it. She just got quietly richer while the internet argued about whether AI would kill creative jobs.
That pattern, invisible people in unsexy industries making real money from AI while the discourse circles around the same ten tech-adjacent profiles, is what I want to talk about. Because the public narrative about who wins from AI is almost completely wrong. And the people getting it wrong are the ones most obsessed with following it.
The narrative we’re sold and why it’s incomplete
Open any major tech publication and the AI wealth story looks like this: AI researchers at labs making $500k. Startup founders who raised on an AI pitch. Developer-influencers selling courses on prompt engineering. A handful of power users who figured out how to automate their entire workflow and now work four hours a day from Bali.
That story isn’t false. Those people exist and some of them are doing extremely well. But it’s a story about a small, visible, already-advantaged group optimizing within a world they already understood.
The more interesting story is happening in the places nobody is looking. AI is creating the largest productivity arbitrage in a generation, and the people capturing it aren’t the ones who understand AI best. They’re the ones who understand their own industry best and happened to pick up one tool at the right moment.
“The biggest winners from AI aren’t the people who know the most about AI. They’re the people who know the most about something else and learned just enough AI to make that something else twice as valuable.”
Who is actually making money right now
I’ve been paying attention to this for about a year, talking to people across different industries, reading between the lines of case studies, and noticing the profiles that keep showing up. The pattern is consistent enough to name.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

None of these people have “AI” in their job title. None of them went viral for a thread about prompt engineering. Most of them would shrug if you asked them what model they’re running. They know what they do. They found a tool that made what they do faster or more valuable. They captured the difference. That’s the whole story.
Why the visible people aren’t the rich ones
There’s a thing that happens in every technology wave. The people who become most visible are the ones who talk about the technology. The people who make the most money are the ones who use the technology. These are rarely the same people.
In the early internet era, the people writing about e-commerce didn’t make as much as the people quietly running e-commerce stores. In the mobile app boom, the people talking about mobile strategy didn’t make as much as the people building and selling apps. AI is following the same pattern, faster.
The AI influencer economy is real but it’s crowded and the margins are compressing. Everyone who figured out that you could sell a course on “how to use ChatGPT for your business” in 2023 is now competing with ten thousand other people who figured out the same thing. The visibility arbitrage is gone. The application arbitrage is very much still there.
The math that most people miss
If you spend 20 hours a week on work that AI can now do in 4 hours, you have two options. Option one: do the same amount of work in less time and keep the savings. Option two: take on three times the clients with the same team. The people getting rich are doing option two or raising prices enough that option one still wins. The people not getting rich are doing the same work in less time and billing the same amount.
The specific industries where this is happening quietly
The industries generating AI wealth right now are not the ones getting the headlines. They’re the ones with high manual overhead, established pricing, and clients who care about outcomes rather than process.
Legal support and paralegal work. A paralegal who can use AI to do contract review, case research, and document summarization at three times the speed is either billing three times the hours or charging a premium for faster turnaround. Both work. The billable hour model in legal was always about time. Now that time is compressible, the people who own the compression own the margin.
Medical documentation and healthcare administration. Doctors spend roughly 37% of their time on documentation, according to a 2024 Annals of Internal Medicine study. The practices that have deployed AI scribe tools are recovering that time. Not all of it, but enough to see significantly more patients without extending hours. The revenue is direct and the math is simple.
Real estate. Agents who have automated their follow-up sequences, property description writing, market analysis reports, and client communication are handling more listings with the same energy. The ones doing this quietly are not advertising it. Their competitors have no idea why their listing turnaround is twice as fast.
Independent consulting and fractional work. A strategy consultant who used to deliver a market analysis in three weeks can now deliver the same quality work in four days. They have three choices: take the same fee and make more per hour, lower the price and win more clients, or take on more clients simultaneously. The smart ones are doing a version of all three depending on the client.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The trait they all share
I said at the start that the people quietly getting rich from AI share one trait. Here it is: they already had a skill that the market valued, and they added AI to it rather than trying to replace their skill with AI.
The wedding photographer didn’t become an AI person. She became a better wedding photographer who uses AI. The bookkeeper didn’t become a tech consultant. She became a more valuable bookkeeper. The paralegal didn’t pivot to a tech career. She became a paralegal who can do the work of three.
This sounds obvious when you say it. It’s apparently not obvious in practice, because the dominant advice in the AI discourse is exactly the reverse: learn AI, add it to your resume, become an AI professional. That advice is creating a massive supply of generalist AI users competing for a much smaller set of roles than people expect.
“Don’t learn AI and then look for a problem to solve. Find the thing you already do better than most people. Then learn just enough AI to do it at a scale or speed that wasn’t possible before.”
The uncomfortable truth about courses and content
There’s a specific type of AI wealth that’s very visible and not very sustainable: the person who made money teaching people how to make money with AI. This isn’t a scam exactly. Some of that content is genuinely useful. But it’s worth understanding the economics.
When someone sells a course on “how to build an AI side hustle,” they are monetizing the gap between what people know and what they think they need to know. That gap closes over time as the information spreads. The course that sold for $497 in 2023 is competing with free YouTube tutorials in 2026. The margins on information products about AI are compressing fast because AI itself is generating the information.
The people getting rich from teaching AI are the ones who got there early and have already moved on to the next thing. If you’re considering buying a course on AI side hustles right now, you’re paying for access to information that was arbitrage twelve months ago and is approaching commodity today.
What actually has lasting value
The skills with durable value in an AI world are judgment, relationships, domain expertise, and the ability to ask the right question before the AI can answer it. None of those are teachable in a three-hour online course. They’re built through years of doing the actual work. AI amplifies these skills enormously. It doesn’t replace them, and it can’t create them from scratch.
The pattern that predicts who wins next
If you want to figure out who’s going to quietly get rich from AI over the next two years, don’t look at who’s most excited about AI. Look at who has the most compressed workflow in an industry with high client demand and established pricing.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

What the wedding photographer actually understood
I keep coming back to the Austin photographer because she understood something that most AI discourse gets wrong. She didn’t ask “how do I use AI?” She asked “where does my time go that doesn’t need to go there?” The answer was editing. So she fixed editing. She didn’t rebuild her entire business around AI. She removed one bottleneck and let the rest of her existing work benefit.
That’s a different question than the one most people are asking. Most people are asking what AI can do. She asked what was slowing her down. The tool came second. The diagnosis came first.
The diagnostic worth running on yourself
Write down the five things you spend the most time on in your work. Then ask, for each one: does this require my specific judgment or expertise, or is it mostly execution? The ones that are mostly execution are the ones AI can compress. The ones that require your specific judgment are the ones that become more valuable when the execution is cheaper. Start with the execution. Protect the judgment.
The people who are quietly getting rich from AI figured this out, most of them intuitively. They didn’t need a framework. They knew their work well enough to see where the time was going and smart enough to try a tool that moved faster. That’s it. No course required. No rebrand as an AI professional. No content strategy about their journey.
Just a skill they already had, running faster than before, billing at the same rate or higher. The math does the rest.
The question isn’t whether AI will change your industry. It already is. The question is whether you’re going to be the person in your field who noticed first — or the one who figured it out by watching someone else’s margin expand while yours stayed flat.
